title: MMALS
summary: A continual-learning research program in which heterogeneous hosts exchange transformed latent signals through an adaptive, auditable medium.
discover: MMALS is a learning ecosystem. Several specialist hosts produce different views of an input. A mediating fungal system decides which signals to transform, combine, preserve, or weaken. The goal is not simply to imitate biology, but to test whether this structure improves continual learning, specialization, and auditability.
engineer: The architecture separates host encoders g_i, host-specific transformations T_i, an inferred context q(c|x), a router r_i(x,c), a mediated latent state z_f, and a readout. Its key engineering distinction from a basic mixture of experts is that the medium is treated as an active computational layer with memory, costs, contribution measurements, and observability.
research: The validated core should remain narrow: inferred-context routing, route-function memory, and energy/stability-aware auditing. Broader claims about biological equivalence, universal continual learning, consciousness, quantum advantage, or host ecology remain separate hypotheses. MMALS is best treated as a falsifiable architecture family plus an evidence discipline.
evidence: Implemented across successive prototypes and later RC branches. Evidence strength differs by mechanism and benchmark; the encyclopedia records this per concept.
limits: MMALS is not established as universally superior to continual-learning baselines, and the biological analogy is not itself evidence.

title: Biological mapping
summary: A disciplined correspondence between host organisms, the fungal medium, and the mycorrhizal exchange relationship.
discover: Trees are the hosts. The mycelial network is the mediating fungal system. Mycorrhiza is the mutualistic exchange interface or relationship between them. These are three different things and must not be drawn as one object.
engineer: In diagrams, host modules produce local representations, the fungal medium transforms and routes signals, and mycorrhizal exchange denotes the governed interaction: contribution, cost, allocation, and reciprocal benefit. The vocabulary prevents the metaphor from obscuring architecture boundaries.
research: This mapping is an editorial and modeling constraint, not a biological claim that artificial modules reproduce fungal physiology. Biological terms should be translated into explicit computational objects and tested through ablations.
evidence: Semantic convention used to keep MMALS articles and visuals internally consistent.
limits: The mapping is explanatory. It cannot establish mechanistic biological equivalence.

title: Host
summary: A specialist computational module whose value is defined by its contribution to the wider MMALS system, not only by isolated accuracy.
discover: A host is one specialist in the ecosystem. It may see the same input as other hosts but learn a different representation.
engineer: Hosts can be heterogeneous encoders, experts, modalities, or reusable functional modules. A host should expose its latent signal, cost, route usage, contribution gain, ablation effect, and drift diagnostics.
research: Host specialization should be established through multiple converging measurements: route entropy and dominance, contribution gain, ablation impact, representation separation, route-function stability, and cross-seed reproducibility.
evidence: Host banks and routing have been implemented since early MMALS prototypes; strong ecological specialization remains mixed and benchmark-dependent.
limits: High route weight alone does not prove functional specialization.

title: Fungal medium
summary: The active mediator that transforms, routes, allocates, remembers, and audits exchanges among hosts.
discover: The medium is not a cable. It decides how signals move and can change them on the way.
engineer: The medium contains host transforms, routing logic, cost terms, memory constraints, context handling, and observability hooks. Its outputs include mediated latent state, route vectors, contribution estimates, and drift signals.
research: The medium becomes scientifically meaningful only when its causal role is separated from extra capacity, generic distillation, replay, freezing, and simple mixture-of-experts effects.
evidence: A true medium with transformations and its own objective was introduced after the earliest structural-routing prototypes.
limits: Calling a softmax gate a fungal medium is insufficient without transformation, objective, and causal diagnostics.

title: Mycorrhizal exchange
summary: The governed mutualistic relationship through which hosts and the fungal medium exchange useful resources or information.
discover: The exchange is mutualistic when using a host genuinely helps the whole system and the interaction does not simply consume resources without benefit.
engineer: A practical proxy removes host i from the mixture and measures the change in loss. Positive contribution gain means the host was useful for that batch or context. Costs can include entropy, latency, energy, memory, or communication.
research: Mutualism is not equivalent to positive average gain in one run. Strong evidence requires longitudinal benefit, resource accounting, causal interventions, and reproducibility across seeds and contexts.
evidence: Contribution-gain terms and ablations have been implemented; robust ecosystem-level mutualism remains an open research target.
limits: The biological word should not hide the fact that gain proxies are model-dependent estimates.

title: Inferred context
summary: A distribution q(c|x) over possible situations inferred from the input instead of supplied as a task identifier.
discover: The system is not told which task it is solving. It first estimates what situation the input belongs to, then chooses routes accordingly.
engineer: The context posterior can be prototype-based, learned, calibrated, blended, or guarded by a global head. Uncertainty in q(c|x) should propagate into route uncertainty and action gating rather than being discarded.
research: Moving from explicit task IDs to inferred context is a central MMALS transition. Evaluation must separate context competence, routing competence, output competence, calibration, and action safety.
evidence: The RC1/RC1b line provided strong controlled evidence on overlapping FashionMNIST contexts, while broader datasets remain under qualification.
limits: A good context posterior on one benchmark does not establish general task-free continual learning.

title: Route
summary: The context- and input-dependent allocation of host contributions used to construct a mediated computation.
discover: A route says how much the system uses each host for the current case.
engineer: Routes lie on a probability simplex. Entropy measures diffuseness; dominance measures concentration; cost-aware routing can include latency, memory, energy, or risk.
research: Route stability is not functional stability. A route can remain numerically similar while host representations or transforms drift.
evidence: Routing is present throughout MMALS, but route-only memory was repeatedly shown to be insufficient as a complete memory object.
limits: Sparse or stable routes do not by themselves prove accuracy, memory, or specialization.

title: Functional route
summary: The composed route-plus-transformation computation, not merely the vector of routing coefficients.
discover: Remembering a road is not enough if the buildings and bridges along that road have changed. MMALS therefore remembers what the route computes.
engineer: A functional route includes route coefficients, host representations, transforms, the mediated latent mixture, and potentially downstream output behavior. Diagnostics separate route drift D_r, latent drift D_z, and output drift D_y.
research: The core non-implication is r_current≈r_teacher does not imply z_f,current≈z_f,teacher. This is a central causal distinction in the v0.7–v0.9 line.
evidence: Mechanistically supported by route-function disentanglement experiments and controlled counterexamples.
limits: Functional preservation can still over-constrain plasticity; it must be evaluated against baselines and resource costs.

title: Functional memory
summary: Memory that preserves a reusable computation or behavior rather than only parameters, topology, or route identity.
discover: The system tries to keep what an old pathway did, not only where the pathway was.
engineer: Functional memory may combine latent distillation, output distillation, route constraints, transform constraints, replay, and snapshots. It should expose both reconstruction fidelity and operational cost.
research: Evidence supports functional memory as more meaningful than route-vector memory in controlled continual-learning settings. The remaining question is when each memory component adds causal value beyond generic replay or distillation.
evidence: Strong internal evidence exists in the v0.7–v0.9 line and later RC2O retention work; benchmark breadth and hardened baselines remain important.
limits: Functional memory is not automatically compact, cheap, or beneficial under all context shifts.

title: Reconstructive and synthetic memory
summary: Two complementary modes: replayable route-function history for audit, and compressed reusable abstractions for efficient operation.
discover: One memory keeps the full story so we can retrace it. The other keeps a compact lesson so the system can act quickly.
engineer: Reconstructive audit memory stores enough evidence to replay or reconstruct route-function paths, while synthetic memory compiles reusable representations, summaries, prototypes, or policies. They have different fidelity, cost, and governance requirements.
research: The key design problem is controlled compilation: determining what can be compressed without losing causal traceability, scientific reproducibility, or safety-relevant context.
evidence: Conceptually integrated into the MMALS roadmap; implementation remains partial across replay, snapshots, prototypes, and evidence bundles.
limits: A compressed memory can hide failure modes; a reconstructive memory can be too expensive for routine inference.

title: Host specialization
summary: Stable, causally meaningful differentiation of host roles across tasks, contexts, or functions.
discover: A host is specialized when it consistently does a particular useful job that other hosts do not simply replace.
engineer: Measure route entropy and dominance, contribution gain, ablation impact, route swaps, representation separation, route-function stability, and cross-seed reproducibility. A single heatmap is not sufficient.
research: Current evidence suggests a mixed ecology with hubs and partners rather than a clean one-host-per-task decomposition. Specialization is therefore an axis of analysis, not a guaranteed architectural outcome.
evidence: Partial support on selected tasks and host pairs; held-out functional and causal evidence has remained mixed in some Geometry-MMALS branches.
limits: Dominance can arise from optimization accidents, capacity imbalance, or router collapse.

title: Probability simplex
summary: The constrained geometric space in which non-negative route weights sum to one.
discover: For three hosts, every route is a point inside a triangle. Each corner means using only one host; the center means using all three equally.
engineer: Simplex geometry makes entropy, dominance, mixtures, barycentric coordinates, and calibrated context posteriors directly visible. Distances should be chosen carefully because Euclidean distance is not always the most meaningful probability geometry.
research: The simplex is a natural base space for routing and probability distributions. Information geometry, optimal transport, and energy formulations provide richer alternatives to naïve Euclidean comparisons.
evidence: Mathematical foundation used by softmax routing and context posteriors.
limits: Representing routes on a simplex does not imply that the underlying latent manifold is simplex-like.

title: Torus
summary: The product of two independent periodic coordinates, S¹×S¹, and a candidate latent geometry only when the data truly carries two periodic degrees of freedom.
discover: Two independent angles make a torus. Each angle wraps around, and together they describe every point on a donut-shaped state space.
engineer: The flat identification square and the 3D donut are two views of the same topology. A toroidal latent model is justified only when two independent periodic variables are present.
research: Toroidal structure can be probed through persistent homology, circular coordinates, reconstruction error, and stability across seeds. Imposing a torus on categorical context would be decorative rather than explanatory.
evidence: Foundational mathematics; any specific MMALS torus claim requires dataset-level geometric evidence.
limits: Visual resemblance to a donut is not evidence of toroidal topology.

title: Stability–plasticity
summary: The tension between preserving useful old functions and adapting enough to learn new ones.
discover: Too little memory causes forgetting. Too much memory prevents learning. A continual learner must live between those extremes.
engineer: Tune memory coefficients with validation-selected policies, report old-task retention and new-task learning separately, and inspect weak seeds. Stability mechanisms include replay, distillation, consolidation, modularity, and route-function preservation.
research: The trade-off is not one-dimensional: context competence, route competence, calibration, compute cost, and action safety can shift the apparent optimum.
evidence: Repeatedly observed across MMALS versions, including negative results where stronger constraints stabilized internal geometry but harmed plasticity.
limits: A single final-average-accuracy score can hide severe task imbalance or poor actionability.

title: CAL evidence layer
summary: A five-axis evaluation layer separating Competence, Coverage, Compactness, Context, and ACTION.
discover: A model can be calibrated without being capable. CAL asks not only whether confidence is statistically valid, but whether the system knows enough to act usefully and safely.
engineer: Competence measures task ability; Coverage checks statistical guarantee; Compactness measures set size or decisiveness; Context measures situation inference; ACTION records whether policy gates permit execution. Calibration can restore coverage but cannot manufacture competence.
research: CAL prevents conformal or calibration guarantees from being over-interpreted. The axes should be reported jointly, with stale-evidence diagnostics and explicit action policies.
evidence: Developed as a reviewer-facing evaluation layer and connected to ongoing continual-learning evidence contracts.
limits: The five axes are a framework; each benchmark still needs precise operational definitions and thresholds.

title: TPUT
summary: The MMALS system-level layer for turning goals, evidence, cost, memory, and routing into governed decisions.
discover: TPUT asks the whole ecosystem what action is worth taking, not merely which model produced the highest score.
engineer: The layer can combine objective state, context posterior, route evidence, memory confidence, compute/latency cost, selector regret, safety gates, and forward/backward control. Keep the operational loop minimal and auditable.
research: TPUT is the place to unify geometry, information, dynamics, access, causal integration, and later embodiment without forcing every branch into the core model.
evidence: A conceptual and specification layer linked to several MMALS branches; full end-to-end implementation is pending.
limits: A system-of-systems layer can become vague unless every signal has a schema, producer, consumer, and decision effect.
